title: Chapter Four: Corpses
content:
## JEONG YEON-SHIN

He did not go to the training ground.

He went to his room, closed the door, sat cross-legged on the bare wooden floor, and began to meditate.

This was different from the Dynamic Technique. That was movement. This was stillness -- drawing the energy of heaven and earth inward through the Baihui acupoint, cycling it through the meridians, letting it settle in the dantian.

*If someone disturbs me and my energy flow gets twisted, I die.*

He thought of his brothers. His father. Doors flung open without knocking, servants sent to interrupt his practice. The assumption that nothing the third son did could possibly matter.

*I do not want to die with my blood vessels knotted like old rope.*

He pushed the thought away. Breathed. Emptied his mind.

The energy came.

It came easily -- too easily. The energy poured through the gate at the crown of his head like water through a broken dam. It filled his meridians, flooded his dantian, pressed against his consciousness with a force that was relentless and impossible to ignore.

He tried to compress it -- to densify the energy the way Yeo Il-shin's had been compressed over centuries. It did not work. The energy would not accept layering.

*Wrong approach? But how else did she achieve that density?*

He put the question aside. Turned his awareness inward.

The blood drained from his face.

*The Baihui acupoint. It's wider than before.*

He had known his crown gate was unusually open. But he had not looked -- really looked -- at it in over a year.

It was enormous.

The energy flowing through his skull was a river, not a stream. The upper dantian was swollen with it, straining.

*How long?*

He calculated. Compared the current flow rate to what he remembered from a year ago. Estimated the rate of expansion.

Five years. Perhaps less. Before his thoughts scattered and his consciousness drowned.

Five years before he died.

The burning at the crown of his head flared -- white-hot, a spike of heat like a nail through his skull. His vision went grey. Three heartbeats of nothing. Then it passed.

He was gasping. Hands flat on the floor, fingertips white against the wood. Sweat on his temples -- the first time his body had produced sweat since the Dynamic Technique reached its peak.

*That has never happened before.*

*Die? Me?*

The word did not feel real. He was fifteen. He was at the start of everything.

Five years.

His hands were shaking. He got up. Grabbed the door handle.

*Fresh air. Think. There must be a solution.*

He opened the door.

The smell hit him first.

Blood. Copper-bright and thick enough to taste. It came with the night air from the direction of the main compound. Human blood. He had butchered enough animals to know the difference.

He picked up his sword. The iron blade, dull-edged, crack in the tang. It was what he had.

He walked. The lanterns along the path were extinguished. The silence was not sleep. It was something finished.

*I already know. I knew the moment I smelled the blood. But I have to see.*

The courtyard of the main building.

Corpses.

They lay where they had fallen. The steward's wife, face-down. Two household guards slumped against the wall, swords half-drawn, throats opened. A serving girl crumpled on the steps.

Each body bore the same wound. A single sword cut to the chest, precise, identical. They had been practicing on the household.

The blood had begun to congeal. Flies crawled across open eyes.

Yeon-shin stood in the courtyard and looked at them.

The still-face held. His face did not change. His breathing did not change.

From beyond the main gate, a boom. The crash of internal energy against internal energy, the shriek of steel on steel. Masters fighting. Real masters, their power shaking the earth.

Yeon-shin walked toward the main building.

Ten men sat in the courtyard, weapons across their knees. Relaxed. The work done.

One turned his head. Large, twin swords crossed on his back, a face that had been broken and reassembled badly.

"Who are you?"

"The third son of this household."

The man stood. Did not draw his swords. He walked toward Yeon-shin and swung his hand -- an open-palmed strike, energy-laden. Casual. The blow a man gives to a child.

Sky-blue light flickered in Yeon-shin's eyes.

He moved. Grabbed the wrist, struck down, twisted. The movements came without thought. He gripped and squeezed.

Bone broke. The sound was sharp and clean, like a green branch snapping. The man screamed.

The others turned.

"What —"

"You —"

Yeon-shin released the wrist. The man staggered back, cradling his arm.

His good fist clenched. He lunged -- a fist technique, the blow swelling with energy.

Sky-blue lightning flashed in Yeon-shin's eyes.

*Head.*

The trajectory was obvious. And in the space between the seeing and the doing, something new ignited inside him.

Intention.

Not circulation. Command. The energy surged through his body, concentrating, densifying, and a name blazed through his consciousness.

*Scripture of Overcoming the Origin.*

It was simply true.

His left foot shattered the ground.

The iron sword cleared the scabbard. The swift sword technique -- the blade drew a line of moonlight through the dark air.

Three heads left three bodies. The arc was over before any of them could do more than widen their eyes.

Their heads hit the ground.

Yeon-shin was already moving. Twisting, pivoting, flowing through the gap. A red-haired woman came at him -- crescent wheel raised, red martial uniform. Blood Flame Sect.

His sword was in her throat before the wheel reached the top of its arc.

He pulled the blade free. She fell forward. He stepped aside and let her drop.

Four dead. Five, counting the broken-wristed man.

His breathing was unchanged. The burning at the crown of his head pulsed. His vision flickered grey. He blinked it clear.

*Not now. Not here.*

*I should feel something. I just killed five people.* The shock was there, packed tight and distant. Later it would come for him.

A thin swordsman stared at him from across the courtyard. Beside him, a giant rose to his feet -- thick as a bear, small bright eyes.

"You've devoted your life to the swift sword," the giant said. His voice was calm. Conversational. "You have no other tricks. Those four were caught off guard. You won't surprise me."

The man was right about the core of it. The swift sword was all he had. One technique. One angle. Against a prepared opponent, it would not be enough.

"Still," the giant said, and there was genuine admiration in his voice, "it's remarkable. Truly remarkable. At your age."

"Blood Flame Sect?" Yeon-shin said, gesturing with his bloody sword toward the red-haired woman at his feet.

"No. I am from the Blade Sect."

Yeon-shin's stomach went cold. The Blade Sect. One of the Thirteen Demons.

"What are two of the Thirteen Demons doing in Xinye County?" he heard himself say.

"None of your business."

"Why are you answering my questions?"

"Because it's a waste." The giant looked at him with something that was almost fondness. "A martial artist as well-trained as you, at your age. It's rare. It's also amusing to hear a countryside boy talk as if he's walked the length of the Central Plains."

*Keep him talking. The Zhongnan masters are fighting outside the gate.*

"A waste?" Yeon-shin let his lips curve. "You're afraid of my maternal family. You know who my grandfather was, and you're afraid of what the Desolate Sect will do if you kill me."

*A gamble. If he calls the bluff —*

The giant's face went dark.

"You crazy brat."

"You cannot kill me," Yeon-shin said. His heart was hammering against his ribs, so hard he was certain the man could hear it. The still-face did not waver. "No matter what I do, no matter how many of your men I cut down, you cannot kill me. Try it and find out what the Desolate Sect does to people who harm their blood."

BOOM.

The main gate exploded.

Splinters and dust. A body came with them -- tumbling, skidding across the flagstones. Blue Taoist robes, scarred cheek. Precipice Sword, bloodied, grey with exhaustion.

Through the ruined gate came Yeo Il-shin and the shorter disciple. His left arm hung broken at his side. And the Elder --

Her left ear was gone. Severed cleanly, blood streaming down her neck. Her face was tight with pain.

*I thought she was a sword immortal.*

Behind them, filling the ruined gate, a man. Huge. Carrying a sword that was less a weapon than a slab of sharpened iron. The energy rolling off him was a pressure Yeon-shin could feel from thirty paces.

He walked with the stride of a man who has never been in a hurry in his life.

The Blade Sect Leader.

The giant before Yeon-shin dropped to his knees, forehead to the ground.

"Sect Leader!"

*Sect Leader. The leader of the Blade Sect. Here. In person.*

Not a raid. A conquest. And this man had broken three Southern Edge masters to achieve it.

The Blade Sect Leader's gaze swept the courtyard and settled on Yeon-shin.

*I am going to die.*

*I was going to die anyway. Five years. So what does it matter?*

He moved.

Not away. Forward.

He sprinted toward the kneeling giant.

"Wha—"

The giant looked up.

Too late.

Yeon-shin's sword punched through his back. The iron blade scraped spine. The giant gasped, slumped. Yeon-shin put one foot on the body and looked up at the Blade Sect Leader.

"Even now," he said.

The sky-blue lightning flickered in his eyes, jade-pale and cold.

"Can you kill me?"
